* identify the main chemical elements in the human body;
* describe and draw basic atomic structure;
* identify the differences between atoms, ions, molecules and compounds;
* distinguish the difference between ionic and covalent bonds;
* define a chemical reaction and identify the difference between an endergonic and exergonic reaction;
* define energy and the units of energy;
* describe the role of ATP within the cell;
* describe the common forms of chemical reactions including synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation and oxidation/reduction reactions;
* outline the functions of water and the chemical property underpinning water’s ability to act as a solvent;
* define the terms mole, molar and molarity;
* define the terms acid, base and salt;
* draw and define the pH scale and describe what is meant by the terms acidic and alkaline;
* describe basic cell structure with particular emphasis to the plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm and mitochondria.

---

## Definition

A foundational chapter scope covering the basic chemistry and cell biology underpinning sport and exercise biochemistry. It encompasses the chemical composition of the human body, atomic and molecular structure, bonding, chemical reactions and energetics, the role of ATP, properties of water, quantitative chemistry (mole/molar/molarity), acid–base chemistry and pH, and the basic structural features of the cell.

## Core Principles

- The human body is composed of a small set of main chemical elements that combine to form the molecules of life.
- Matter is organised hierarchically: atoms (with a defined basic atomic structure) combine into ions, molecules, and compounds.
- Chemical bonds include ionic bonds (transfer of electrons) and covalent bonds (sharing of electrons), with distinct properties.
- A chemical reaction involves rearrangement of atoms; reactions can be classified energetically as endergonic (energy-requiring) or exergonic (energy-releasing).
- Energy is the capacity to do work and is measured in defined units of energy.
- ATP functions as the principal energy currency within the cell, coupling exergonic to endergonic processes.
- Common reaction types include synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation, and oxidation/reduction reactions.
- Water’s polar nature underpins its ability to act as a solvent and supports its diverse biological functions.
- Quantitative chemistry is expressed via the mole, molar, and molarity.
- Acids, bases, and salts are defined by their behaviour in solution; the pH scale quantifies acidity and alkalinity.
- The cell’s basic structure includes the plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, and mitochondria, each with characteristic roles.

## Practical Applications

- Provides the chemical and cellular foundation required to understand metabolism, bioenergetics, and exercise biochemistry.
- Underlies interpretation of ATP turnover and energy transfer during physical activity.
- Supports understanding of acid–base balance (pH) relevant to exercise physiology.
- Enables quantitative work in the laboratory through mole/molar/molarity concepts.
- Frames understanding of cellular compartments (e.g., mitochondria) as sites of energy metabolism.

## Related Concepts

- Bioenergetics
- Cell structure (plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria)
- Acid–base chemistry / pH
- Oxidation–reduction reactions
- Phosphorylation / dephosphorylation
- Water as a biological solvent
